title.zh: 卷八十 宣元六王傳
title.en: Volume 80: The six sons of Emperors Xuan and Yuan
title.raw: 卷八十 宣元六王傳 Volume 80: The six sons of Emperors Xuan and Yuan

sentences.zh: 卷八十 宣元六王傳第五十

translations.literal: Volume 80: Biographies of the Six Princes of Emperors Xuan and Yuan, Number 50.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 80: Biographies of the Six Princes of Emperors Xuan and Yuan (no. 50).

sentences.zh: 孝宣皇帝五男

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoxuan had five sons.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan had five sons.

sentences.zh: 孝宣皇帝五男。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoxuan had five sons.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan had five sons.

sentences.zh: 許皇后生孝元帝，張婕妤生淮陽憲王欽，衛婕妤生楚孝王囂，公孫婕妤生東平思王宇，戎婕妤生中山哀王竟。

translations.literal: Empress Xu bore Emperor Xiaoyuan; Lady Zhang Jieyu bore Prince Xian of Huaiyang, Qin; Lady Wei Jieyu bore Prince Xiao of Chu, Xiao; Lady Gongsun Jieyu bore Prince Si of Dongping, Yu; Lady Rong Jieyu bore Prince Ai of Zhongshan, Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Xu gave birth to Emperor Yuan. Lady Zhang the Honored Lady bore Prince Xian of Huaiyang, Qin; Lady Wei the Honored Lady bore Prince Xiao of Chu, Xiao; Lady Gongsun the Honored Lady bore Prince Si of Dongping, Yu; and Lady Rong the Honored Lady bore Prince Ai of Zhongshan, Jing.

sentences.zh: 淮陽憲王欽

translations.literal: Prince Xian of Huaiyang, Qin
translations.idiomatic: Prince Xian of Huaiyang, Qin

sentences.zh: 淮陽憲王欽，元康三年立，母張婕妤有寵於宣帝。

translations.literal: Prince Xian of Huaiyang, Qin was enfeoffed in the third year of Yuankang; his mother Lady Zhang Jieyu was favored by Emperor Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Xian of Huaiyang, Qin, was established in the third year of Yuankang (65 BCE). His mother, Lady Zhang the Honored Lady, enjoyed Emperor Xuan’s favor.

sentences.zh: 霍皇后廢後，上欲立張婕妤為后。

translations.literal: After Empress Huo was deposed, the emperor wished to make Lady Zhang Jieyu empress.
translations.idiomatic: After Empress Huo was cast aside, the emperor meant to raise Lady Zhang to empress.

sentences.zh: 久之，懲艾霍氏欲害皇太子，乃更選後宮無子而謹慎者，乃立長陵王婕妤為后，令母養太子。

translations.literal: After a long time, chastened by the Huo clan’s attempt to harm the heir apparent, he instead chose from the rear palace a woman who had borne no sons and was careful and modest, and established Lady Wang Jieyu of Changling as empress, ordering her to mother the heir.
translations.idiomatic: In time, mindful of how the Huo family had tried to harm the crown prince, he looked elsewhere among his childless, cautious consorts and made Lady Wang of Changling empress, charging her with raising the heir.

sentences.zh: 後無寵，希御見，唯張婕妤最幸。

translations.literal: Afterward she had no favor and rarely attended him; only Lady Zhang Jieyu was most favored.
translations.idiomatic: The new empress soon lost his attention and seldom saw him; Lady Zhang alone remained his favorite.

sentences.zh: 而憲王壯大，好經書、法律，聰達有材，帝甚愛之。

translations.literal: The Prince Xian grew to adulthood, loved the classics and statutes, was intelligent and talented, and the emperor loved him greatly.
translations.idiomatic: As the Prince of Huaiyang came of age, he took to the classics and law, proved quick and capable, and won the emperor’s deep affection.

sentences.zh: 太子寬仁，喜儒術，上數嗟歎憲王，輔曰：「真我子也！」

translations.literal: The heir apparent was generous and humane and delighted in Confucian learning; the emperor often sighed in admiration of the Prince Xian, saying, “He is truly my son!”
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince was mild and humane and loved Confucian scholarship; the emperor would sigh over Prince Xian and tell his attendants, “Now that is my son!”

sentences.zh: 常有意欲立張婕妤與憲王，然用太子起於微細，上少依倚許氏，及即位而許后以殺死，太子蚤失母，故弗忍也。

translations.literal: He often had the intention of establishing Lady Zhang Jieyu and the Prince Xian, but because the heir had risen from low beginnings, in youth he had relied on the Xu family, and when he took the throne Empress Xu had died by execution, and the heir had early lost his mother, therefore he could not bear it.
translations.idiomatic: He often toyed with making Lady Zhang and the prince his succession, yet the crown prince had come up from nothing: as a young man the emperor had leaned on the Xu clan, and after his accession Empress Xu had been executed, leaving the boy motherless—so he could not bring himself to replace him.

sentences.zh: 久之，上以故丞相韋賢子玄成陽狂讓侯兄，經明行高，稱於朝廷，乃召拜玄成為淮陽中尉，欲感諭憲王，輔以推讓之臣，由是太子遂安。

translations.literal: After a long time, because the former Chancellor Wei Xian’s son Xuancheng feigned madness to yield the marquisate to his elder brother, was clear in the classics and high in conduct, and was praised at court, he summoned and appointed Xuancheng as Commandant of Huaiyang, wishing to instruct and admonish the Prince Xian and support him with a minister who practiced yielding; thereby the heir apparent was secured.
translations.idiomatic: Years later he recalled how Wei Xuancheng, son of the former chancellor Wei Xian, had played mad to yield his noble title to an elder brother—learned in the classics and renowned for integrity—and summoned him to serve as commandant in Huaiyang, hoping such an example of deference would steady the ambitious prince and quiet the succession.

sentences.zh: 宣帝崩，元帝即位，乃遣憲王之國。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xuan died and Emperor Yuan took the throne, he then sent the Prince Xian to his kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: When Xuan died and Yuan ascended, the new emperor sent Prince Qin to his fief at Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 時，張婕妤已卒，憲王有外祖母，舅張博兄弟三人歲至淮陽見親，輒受王賜。

translations.literal: At that time Lady Zhang Jieyu had already died; the Prince Xian had a maternal grandmother, and his uncles Zhang Bo and his two brothers each year came to Huaiyang to visit kin and received gifts from the king.
translations.idiomatic: By then Lady Zhang was dead. The prince still had kin on his mother’s side: each year his uncles Zhang Bo and his two brothers visited Huaiyang and went home laden with the king’s largesse.

sentences.zh: 後王上書，請徙外家張氏於國。

translations.literal: Later the king submitted a memorial asking to move his maternal Zhang kin into the kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: Later the prince petitioned to bring his mother’s Zhang relatives to live in his state.

sentences.zh: 博上書，願留守墳墓，獨不徙。

translations.literal: Bo submitted a memorial wishing to remain to guard the tombs alone and not move.
translations.idiomatic: Bo memorialized that he wished to stay behind to tend the family graves and not relocate.

sentences.zh: 王恨之。

translations.literal: The king resented him.
translations.idiomatic: The prince nursed a grudge against him.

sentences.zh: 後博至淮陽，王賜之少。

translations.literal: Later when Bo came to Huaiyang, the king gave him little.
translations.idiomatic: When Bo next arrived in Huaiyang, the king’s gifts were stingy.

sentences.zh: 博言：「負責數百萬，願王為償。」

translations.literal: Bo said, “I owe several million in debt; I wish the king would repay it for me.”
translations.idiomatic: Bo said, “I am several million cash in debt; I need the king to cover it.”

sentences.zh: 王不許，博辭去，令弟光恐云王遇大人益解，博欲上書為大人乞骸骨去。

translations.literal: The king did not permit it; Bo departed, ordering his younger brother Guang to frighten him, saying the king’s treatment of elders was growing colder, and Bo wished to submit a memorial asking permission for the elder to retire on grounds of bones.
translations.idiomatic: The king refused. As Bo left, he told his younger brother Guang to worry the king with word that his lordship was growing cold toward his elders and that Bo meant to memorialize for permission to retire—unless something changed.

sentences.zh: 王乃遣人持黃金五十斤送博。

translations.literal: The king then sent a man with fifty jin of gold to deliver to Bo.
translations.idiomatic: The prince then dispatched an agent with fifty jin of gold for Bo.

sentences.zh: 博喜，還書謝，為諂語盛稱譽王，因言：「當今朝廷無賢臣，災變數見，足為寒心。

translations.literal: Bo was pleased, returned a letter of thanks, used flattering language greatly praising the king, and therefore said, “At present the court has no worthy ministers; disasters and anomalies appear repeatedly—enough to chill the heart.
translations.idiomatic: Bo was delighted and wrote back in fulsome praise, adding, “The court today is bereft of good men; portents and disasters pile up—it is enough to freeze one’s blood.

sentences.zh: 萬姓咸歸望於大王，大王奈何恬然不求入朝見，輔助主上乎？」

translations.literal: The myriad common people all turn their hopes to the great king; how can the great king remain indifferent and not seek to enter court and assist the lord above?”
translations.idiomatic: The people are looking to you, my lord—how can you sit idle and not press for an audience to steady the throne?”

sentences.zh: 使弟光數說王宜聽博計，令於京師說用事貴人為王求朝。

translations.literal: He had his younger brother Guang repeatedly urge the king that he ought to follow Bo’s plan and have him speak to powerful men at the capital who handle affairs to seek an audience for the king.
translations.idiomatic: He had Guang keep pressing the prince to follow his scheme and let Bo lobby the great men at Chang’an who held the levers of power.

sentences.zh: 許不納其言。

translations.literal: His words were not accepted.
translations.idiomatic: The prince would not heed him.

sentences.zh: 後光欲至長安，辭王，復言「願盡力與博共為王求朝。

translations.literal: Later Guang wished to go to Chang’an, took leave of the king, and again said, “I wish to exert myself together with Bo to seek an audience for the king.
translations.idiomatic: When Guang later prepared to leave for Chang’an, he again assured the king, “Bo and I will do everything we can to win you a summons.

sentences.zh: 王即日至長安，可因平陽侯。」

translations.literal: If the king arrives in Chang’an the same day, you may do so through the Marquis of Pingyang.”
translations.idiomatic: Once you reach the capital, the Marquis of Pingyang can open the door for you.”

sentences.zh: 光得王欲求朝語，馳使人語博。

translations.literal: Guang obtained the king’s words desiring an audience and galloped a messenger to tell Bo.
translations.idiomatic: Guang carried the prince’s agreement to Bo by fast courier.

sentences.zh: 博知王意動，復遺王書曰：「博幸得肺腑，數進愚策，未見省察。

translations.literal: Bo knew the king’s mind was moved and again sent the king a letter saying, “Bo has fortunately been as close as lungs and liver, several times advancing foolish plans without having been examined.
translations.idiomatic: Bo saw the hook take and wrote again: “I have presumed on our kinship and offered counsel again and again, yet you have never truly weighed it.

sentences.zh: 北遊燕、趙，欲循行郡國求幽隱之士，聞齊有駟先生者，善為《司馬兵法》，大將之材也，博得謁見，承間進問五帝、三王究竟要道，卓爾非世俗之所知。

translations.literal: I traveled north through Yan and Zhao, wishing to tour the commanderies and kingdoms seeking reclusive gentlemen; I heard that in Qi there is Master Si who is good at the Sima fa on war—material for a great general—and Bo was able to meet him and, taking an opening, advanced questions on the essential way of the Five Emperors and Three Kings—outstanding, not what the vulgar know.
translations.idiomatic: I have ridden through Yan and Zhao seeking hidden worthies and learned of Master Si in Qi, master of the Sima fa—true material for a grand captain. I gained an audience and, when the moment allowed, pressed him on the deepest doctrine of the Five Emperors and Three Kings—insights this world seldom hears.

sentences.zh: 今邊境不安，天下騷動，微此人其莫能安也。

translations.literal: Now the borders are not secure and the realm is in turmoil; without this man perhaps none can bring peace.
translations.idiomatic: The frontier is unquiet and the empire roiled; without such a man there may be no peace.

sentences.zh: 又聞北海之瀕有賢人焉，累世不可逮，然難致也。

translations.literal: I also heard that on the shore of the Northern Sea there is a worthy man, generations cannot match him, yet he is hard to summon.
translations.idiomatic: They say another sage lives by the northern sea, unequalled for generations though nearly impossible to draw out.

sentences.zh: 得此二人而薦之，功亦不細矣。

translations.literal: To obtain these two men and recommend them would also be no small achievement.
translations.idiomatic: To present both to the throne would be no small service.

sentences.zh: 博願馳西以此赴助漢急，無財幣以通顯之。

translations.literal: Bo wishes to gallop west with this to aid the Han in its urgency, but has no wealth or currency to gain access for them.
translations.idiomatic: I would ride west tomorrow to serve the dynasty in its straits, but I lack the gold to open the right doors.

sentences.zh: 趙王使謁者持牛、酒，黃金三十斤勞博，博不受；

translations.literal: The King of Zhao sent his usher with cattle, wine, and thirty jin of gold to reward Bo; Bo did not accept;
translations.idiomatic: The king of Zhao sent wine, oxen, and thirty jin of gold; Bo declined them;

sentences.zh: 復使人願尚女，聘金二百斤，博未許。

translations.literal: again he sent a man wishing to give a daughter in marriage with bride-price of two hundred jin, and Bo had not yet consented.
translations.idiomatic: he then offered a daughter in marriage with two hundred jin as bride-price, and Bo still hesitated.

sentences.zh: 會得光書云大王已遣光西，與博並力求朝。

translations.literal: Just then he received Guang’s letter saying the great king had already sent Guang west to join Bo in striving together for an audience.
translations.idiomatic: Then your letter arrived, my lord, saying you had sent Guang west to join me in pressing for your audience.

sentences.zh: 博自以棄捐，不意大王還意反義，結以硃顏，願殺身報德。

translations.literal: Bo considered himself cast aside and did not expect the great king to turn his regard back in righteousness and bind him with crimson countenance; he wishes to kill himself to repay virtue.
translations.idiomatic: I thought myself forgotten; I never dreamed you would honor me again and bind me with such kindness. I would lay down my life to repay it.

sentences.zh: 朝事何足言！

translations.literal: What is there to say about the matter of an audience!
translations.idiomatic: The audience itself is the least of it!

sentences.zh: 大王誠賜咳唾，使得盡死，湯、禹所以成大功也。

translations.literal: If the great king truly grants a cough or spittle so that I may exhaust my death, that is how Tang and Yu accomplished great merit.
translations.idiomatic: If my lord will only breathe a word of consent, I will spend my last breath as Tang and Yu did to build great deeds.

sentences.zh: 駟先生蓄積道術，書無不有，願知大王所好，請得輒上。」

translations.literal: Master Si has stored up the way and techniques; there is no book he does not have; I wish to know what the great king favors, and I ask to be able to present it at once.”
translations.idiomatic: Master Si’s library holds every text under heaven; tell me your interests and I will lay the right volumes before you at once.”

sentences.zh: 王得書喜說，報博書曰：「子高乃幸左顧存恤，發心惻隱，顯至誠，納以嘉謀，語以至事，雖亦不敏，敢不諭意！

translations.literal: The king received the letter and was pleased, and replied to Bo’s letter saying, “Zi Gao has deigned to look askance and show care, arousing compassion in the heart, displaying utmost sincerity, accepting fine plans, speaking of the greatest affairs—though I am also not sharp, how dare I fail to understand the intention!
translations.idiomatic: The prince was delighted and answered: “Zi Gao, you bend down to pity me, speak from the heart, offer wise counsel and weighty truth—dull though I am, how could I fail to grasp your meaning?

sentences.zh: 今遣有司為子高償責二百萬。」

translations.literal: Now I dispatch the responsible officials to repay Zi Gao’s debt of two million.”
translations.idiomatic: I have ordered my officers to settle your two-million debt this day.”

sentences.zh: 是時，博女婿京房以明《易》陰陽得幸於上，數召見言事。

translations.literal: At this time Bo’s son-in-law Jing Fang, through clarity in the Changes and yin and yang, had gained the emperor’s favor and was several times summoned to speak on affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Bo’s son-in-law Jing Fang had won favor by expounding the Book of Changes and yin–yang theory and was repeatedly called to advise the throne.

sentences.zh: 自謂為石顯、五鹿充宗所排，謀不得用，數為博道之。

translations.literal: He claimed that he was squeezed out by Shi Xian and Wulu Chongzong and his plans could not be used, and several times spoke of this to Bo.
translations.idiomatic: He complained that Shi Xian and Wulu Chongzong had blocked him and that none of his policies were adopted, and he poured this out to Bo again and again.

sentences.zh: 博常欲誑耀淮陽王，即具記房諸所說災異及召見密語，持予淮陽王以為信驗，詐言：「已見中書令石君求朝，許以金五百斤。

translations.literal: Bo always wished to dazzle the King of Huaiyang, and so fully recorded all that Fang had said about disasters and anomalies and secret words at audiences, and took them to give to the King of Huaiyang as proof, falsely saying, “I have already seen Palace Secretary Shi and sought an audience; he promised it for five hundred jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: Bo meant to dazzle the Prince of Huaiyang, so he copied every tale Fang had told of portents and palace secrets and presented them as proof, lying that he had already secured Shi Xian’s promise of an audience for five hundred jin of gold.

sentences.zh: 賢聖制事，蓋慮功而不計費。

translations.literal: The sages in ordering affairs surely weigh merit and do not reckon expense.
translations.idiomatic: The sages weighed outcomes, not price tags.

sentences.zh: 昔禹治鴻水，百姓罷勞，成功既立，萬世賴之。

translations.literal: In antiquity Yu controlled the flood waters; the common people were exhausted by toil; when the achievement was established, myriad generations relied on it.
translations.idiomatic: When Yu tamed the flood, the people wore themselves to the bone, yet once the work was done, countless ages lived by it.

sentences.zh: 今聞陛下春秋未滿四十，發齒墮落，太子幼弱，佞人用事，陰陽不調，百姓疾疫饑饉死者且半，鴻水之害殆不過此。

translations.literal: Now I hear that Your Majesty’s years are not yet forty, hair and teeth are falling, the heir is young and weak, crafty men hold power, yin and yang are not in harmony, and among the common people plague and famine have killed nearly half; the harm of the flood waters is perhaps no worse than this.
translations.idiomatic: They say you are not yet forty, yet your hair and teeth fail, the heir is a child, favorites run the court, heaven and earth are out of tune, and plague and famine have carried off perhaps half the realm—no less dire than Yu’s deluge.

sentences.zh: 大王緒欲救世，將比功德，何可以忽？

translations.literal: The great king in succession wishes to save the age; compared with that merit, how can this be neglected?
translations.idiomatic: You mean to save the age, my lord—set that deed beside a few hundred jin of gold.

sentences.zh: 博已與大儒知道者為大王為便宜奏，陳安危，指災異，大王朝見，先口陳其意而後奏之，上必大說。

translations.literal: Bo has already joined with great Confucians who know the way to draft for the great king a convenient memorial stating safety and danger, pointing to disasters and anomalies; when the great king has his audience, first declare the intent orally and then submit it, and the emperor will surely be greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: I have lined up learned men to draft a memorial on peril and portent; when you face the throne, speak the heart of it aloud, then hand up the text, and the Son of Heaven will be overjoyed.

sentences.zh: 事成功立，大王即有周、邵之名，邪臣散亡，公卿變節，功德亡比，而梁、趙之寵必歸大王，外家亦將富貴，何復望大王之金錢？」

translations.literal: When the affair succeeds and the achievement is established, the great king will then have the names of the dukes of Zhou and Shao, wicked ministers will scatter and flee, dukes and ministers will change their ways, merit without compare—and the favor shown Liang and Zhao will surely return to the great king, and the maternal kin will also become rich and noble; why look again to the great king’s gold and cash?”
translations.idiomatic: Success would give you the stature of the Duke of Zhou and the Duke of Shao, sweep the sycophants from power, and turn every minister’s coat—while the favor now lavished on Liang and Zhao would fall to you and your wife’s clan. Why haggle over gold?”

sentences.zh: 王喜說，報博書曰：「乃者詔下，止諸侯朝者，寡人憯然不知所出。

translations.literal: The king was pleased and replied to Bo’s letter saying, “Recently an edict came down forbidding the feudal kings from attending court; I was dismayed and did not know what to do.
translations.idiomatic: The prince wrote back gladly: “When the edict lately barred princes from audience, I was at a loss.

sentences.zh: 子高素有顏、冉之資，臧武之智，子貢之辯，卞莊子之勇，兼此四者，世之所鮮。

translations.literal: Zi Gao has always had the endowment of Yan and Ran, the wisdom of Zang Wuzhong, the eloquence of Zigong, and the courage of Bian Zhuangzi; to combine these four is rare in the world.
translations.idiomatic: You have Yan Hui’s and Ran Boniu’s gifts, Zang Wuzhong’s wit, Zigong’s tongue, and Bian Zhuangzi’s nerve—four virtues seldom met in one man.

sentences.zh: 既開端緒，願卒成之。

translations.literal: Since the beginning has been opened, I wish to see it through to the end.
translations.idiomatic: You have opened the way; see it through with me.

sentences.zh: 求朝，義事也，奈何行金錢乎！」

translations.literal: To seek an audience is a righteous affair; how can one traffic in gold and cash!”
translations.idiomatic: Seeking an audience is a matter of right—how can we buy it with money!”

sentences.zh: 博報曰：「已許石君，須以成事。」

translations.literal: Bo replied saying, “I have already promised Secretary Shi; we must use it to complete the affair.”
translations.idiomatic: Bo answered, “I have given Shi my word—we need the gold to finish this.”

sentences.zh: 王以金五百斤予博。

translations.literal: The king gave Bo five hundred jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: The prince handed over five hundred jin of gold.

sentences.zh: 會房出為郡守，離左右，顯具有此事告之。

translations.literal: Just then Fang went out as governor of a commandery, left the emperor’s side, and Shi Xian fully reported this matter to him.
translations.idiomatic: Then Jing Fang was posted away from court, and Shi Xian laid the whole intrigue before the emperor.

sentences.zh: 房漏洩省中語，博兄弟詿誤諸侯王，誹謗政治，狡猾不道，皆下獄。

translations.literal: Fang leaked words from within the palace; Bo and his brothers misled the feudal kings, slandered the government, and were crafty and contrary to the Way; all were cast into prison.
translations.idiomatic: Fang had divulged palace secrets; Bo and his brothers had led a prince astray and defamed the state—charges of wicked irreverence—and all were clapped in jail.

sentences.zh: 有司奏請逮捕欽，上不忍致法，遣諫大夫王駿賜欽璽書曰：「皇帝問淮陽王。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized requesting Qin’s arrest; the emperor could not bear to apply the law and sent Grandee Remonstrant Wang Jun to bestow on Qin an imperial missive saying, “The emperor inquires of the King of Huaiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Officials asked to arrest Prince Qin; the emperor relented and sent Grandee Remonstrant Wang Jun with a sealed edict: “The Son of Heaven addresses the King of Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 有司奏王，王舅張博數遺王書，非毀政治，謗訕天子，褒舉諸侯，稱引周、湯，以諂惑王，所言尤惡，悖逆無道。

translations.literal: The responsible officials have reported on the king: the king’s uncle Zhang Bo several times sent letters to the king, destroying the government, reviling the Son of Heaven, praising the feudal kings, citing Zhou and Tang, thereby flattering and misleading the king; his words were especially vile, rebellious and contrary to the Way.
translations.idiomatic: They say your uncle Zhang Bo wrote you again and again, attacking policy, mocking the throne, praising other kings, and invoking the ancient sages only to seduce you—language foul enough to count as treason.

sentences.zh: 王不舉奏而多與金錢，報以好言，罪至不赦，朕惻焉不忍聞，為王傷之。

translations.literal: The king did not report it but gave much gold and cash and replied with fine words; the crime reaches to unpardonable; We are pained and cannot bear to hear it, and We grieve for the king.
translations.idiomatic: You never denounced him yet showered him with gold and answered in honeyed words—guilt that admits no pardon. I shrink from hearing it and grieve for you.

sentences.zh: 推原厥本，不祥自博，惟王之心，匪同於凶。

translations.literal: Tracing to the root of the origin, ill auspice came from Bo alone; as for the king’s heart, it is not the same as the vicious.
translations.idiomatic: Trace the evil to its source and it begins with Bo alone; your own heart was never that of a traitor.

sentences.zh: 已詔有司勿治王事，遣諫大夫駿申諭朕意。

translations.literal: I have already ordered the responsible officials not to handle the king’s affair, and sent Grandee Remonstrant Jun to declare Our intention.
translations.idiomatic: I have told my officers not to proceed against you and sent Grandee Remonstrant Jun to make my meaning plain.

sentences.zh: 《詩》不云乎：『靖恭爾位，正直是與』？

translations.literal: Does not the Odes say, “Be reverent in your station; align with the upright”?
translations.idiomatic: Does not the Classic of Poetry say, “Quiet and reverent in your place; keep company with the upright”?

sentences.zh: 王其勉之！」

translations.literal: Let the king strive in this!”
translations.idiomatic: Strive to live by that, my cousin!”

sentences.zh: 駿諭指曰：「禮為諸侯制相朝聘之義，蓋以考禮一德，尊事天子也。

translations.literal: Jun declared the meaning saying, “By ritual the feudal kings have regulated the meaning of mutual court visits, in order to examine ritual and unify virtue and honoringly serve the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Jun explained: “Ritual sets how kings attend court—to harmonize conduct with the Way and honor the Son of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 且王不學《詩》乎？

translations.literal: Moreover has the king not studied the Odes?
translations.idiomatic: Have you not read the Odes yourself?

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：『俾侯於魯，為周室輔。』

translations.literal: The Odes says, “Made marquis in Lu, to be a buttress for the house of Zhou.”
translations.idiomatic: It says, “They made him marquis in Lu, a pillar for the house of Zhou.”

sentences.zh: 今王舅博數遺王書，所言悖逆。

translations.literal: Now the king’s uncle Bo several times sent letters to the king; what he said was rebellious.
translations.idiomatic: Yet your uncle Bo wrote you repeatedly with treasonous counsel.

sentences.zh: 王幸受詔策，通經術，知諸侯名譽不當出竟。

translations.literal: The king has fortunately received edicts and orders, comprehends the classics, and knows that a feudal king’s reputation ought not to extend beyond his borders.
translations.idiomatic: You have received the imperial charge, you know the classics, and you know a king’s name must not cross his borders.

sentences.zh: 天子普覆，德佈於朝，而恬有博言，多予金錢，與相報應，不忠莫大焉。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven’s cover is universal; virtue spreads at court—yet you calmly harbored Bo’s words, gave much gold and cash, and answered in mutual correspondence; no disloyalty is greater.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor’s grace covers all; his virtue fills the court—yet you swallowed Bo’s plots, paid him in gold, and wrote back as if in league. Nothing is more disloyal.

sentences.zh: 故事，諸侯王獲罪京師，罪惡輕重，縱不伏誅，必蒙遷削貶黜之罪，未有但已者也。

translations.literal: By precedent, when feudal kings incur guilt at the capital, whether the crime is light or heavy, even if they are not put to death, they surely suffer the punishment of removal, demotion, or dismissal; there has never been a case of stopping there alone.
translations.idiomatic: By precedent a prince who sins at the capital, whether the fault is great or small, faces exile or stripping of rank if not the axe—never a simple shrug.

sentences.zh: 今聖主赦王之罪，又憐王失計忘本，為博所惑，加賜璽書，使諫大夫申諭至意，殷勤之恩，豈有量哉！

translations.literal: Now the sage lord has pardoned the king’s crime and also pities the king for losing his bearings and forgetting his roots, being misled by Bo, and has added the gift of an imperial missive and sent a grandee remonstrant to declare the utmost intention—can the depth of this earnest favor be measured?
translations.idiomatic: Yet he forgives you, pities you for forgetting who you are under Bo’s spell, and adds this personal edict and an envoy’s voice—measure that kindness if you can.

sentences.zh: 博等所犯惡大，群下之所共攻，王法之所不赦也。

translations.literal: What Bo and the others committed is greatly vile; it is what all below jointly attack and what kingly law does not pardon.
translations.idiomatic: Bo’s crimes are monstrous; every minister condemns them and the law cannot spare them.

sentences.zh: 自今以來，王毋復以博等累心，務與眾棄之。

translations.literal: From now on, let the king never again let Bo and the others burden his mind; strive together with the multitude to cast them off.
translations.idiomatic: From this day do not let Bo trouble your thoughts again; join the world in casting him off.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》之義，大能變改。

translations.literal: The meaning of the Spring and Autumn is that the great can change and reform.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn Annals teach that even grave fault may be redeemed by change.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰『借用白茅，無咎』，言臣子之道，改過自新，潔己以承上，然後免於咎也。

translations.literal: The Changes says, “Borrowing white grass, no blame,” meaning the way of ministers and sons: to reform faults and renew oneself, to purify oneself to receive what is above, and then one escapes blame.
translations.idiomatic: The Changes speaks of laying white rushes beneath an offering—no blame—meaning a son of the house may cleanse himself, turn anew, and serve his lord without fault.

sentences.zh: 王其留意慎戒，惟思所以悔過易行，塞重責，稱厚恩者。

translations.literal: Let the king take care and be on guard; only think how to repent faults and change conduct, block heavy responsibility, and match thick favor.
translations.idiomatic: Guard your every step; think how to mend your ways, meet this mercy, and silence heavier blame.

sentences.zh: 如此，則長有富貴，社稷安矣。」

translations.literal: If so, then you will long possess wealth and nobility and the altars of soil and grain will be secure.”
translations.idiomatic: Do that, and you keep rank and fortune, and the state altars stand firm.”

sentences.zh: 於是淮陽王欽免冠稽首謝曰：「奉籓無狀，過惡暴列，陛下不忍致法，加大恩，遣使者申諭道術守籓之義。

translations.literal: Thereupon the King of Huaiyang, Qin, removed his cap and knocked his head in thanks saying, “In holding the screen I have lacked deportment; my faults have been exposed in a row; Your Majesty could not bear to apply the law and has extended great favor, dispatching envoys to explain the meaning of the way and of guarding the screen.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Qin doffed his cap and kowtowed: “I have disgraced my charge as a bulwark; my crimes lie bare. You would not punish me but sent envoys to teach the duty of a vassal.

sentences.zh: 伏念博罪惡尤深，當伏重誅。

translations.literal: I reflect that Bo’s crime is especially deep and ought to suffer heavy execution.
translations.idiomatic: Bo’s guilt is the deeper and deserves death.

sentences.zh: 臣欽願悉心自新，奉承詔策。

translations.literal: Your subject Qin wishes to give his whole heart to renewing himself and to receive and carry out the edicts and orders.
translations.idiomatic: I, Qin, will devote myself anew to obeying your command.

sentences.zh: 頓首死罪。」

translations.literal: I knock my head for the crime deserving death.”
translations.idiomatic: I kowtow for a crime worthy of death.”

sentences.zh: 京房及博兄弟三人皆棄市，妻子徙邊。

translations.literal: Jing Fang and Bo and his two brothers were all exposed in the market; wives and children were moved to the border.
translations.idiomatic: Jing Fang and Zhang Bo’s brothers were executed in the marketplace; their families were banished to the frontier.

sentences.zh: 三十六年薨。

translations.literal: In the thirty-sixth year he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the thirty-sixth year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 子文王玄嗣，二十六年薨。

translations.literal: His son King Wen Xuan succeeded; in the twenty-sixth year he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Prince Wen, Xuan, succeeded and died in the twenty-sixth year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 子縯嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: His son Yan succeeded; at the time of Wang Mang the line was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: His son Yan inherited the title; the line ended under Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 楚孝王囂

translations.literal: Prince Xiao of Chu, Xiao
translations.idiomatic: Prince Xiao of Chu, Xiao

sentences.zh: 初，成帝時又立紆弟景為定陶王。

translations.literal: At the beginning, under Emperor Cheng, they again established Jing, younger brother of Yu, as King of Dingtao.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, under Emperor Cheng, Jing—Yu’s younger brother—had been made king of Dingtao.

sentences.zh: 廣戚侯勳薨，謚曰煬侯，子顯嗣。

translations.literal: Marquis of Guangqi Xun died; his posthumous title was Marquis Yang; his son Xian succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: The marquis of Guangqi, Xun, died with the posthumous title Yang; his son Xian succeeded.

sentences.zh: 平帝崩，無子，王莽立顯子嬰為孺子，奉平帝后。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ping died without a son, Wang Mang established Xian’s son Ying as the Infant, to serve Empress Dowager Ping.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Ping died childless, Wang Mang set Xian’s son Ying on the throne as the infant heir to carry on Ping’s line.

sentences.zh: 莽篡位，以嬰為定安公。

translations.literal: When Mang usurped the throne, he made Ying Duke of Ding’an.
translations.idiomatic: When Mang seized the throne he demoted Ying to duke of Ding’an.

sentences.zh: 漢既誅莽，更始時嬰在長安，平陵方望等頗知天文，以為更始必敗，嬰本統當立者也，共起兵將嬰至臨涇，立為天子。

translations.literal: When Han had executed Mang, at the time of Gengshi Ying was in Chang’an; Fang Wang and others at Pingling, who knew astronomy somewhat, thought Gengshi would surely fail and that Ying by original succession ought to have been established; they jointly raised troops and brought Ying to Linjing and established him as Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: After Han destroyed Wang Mang, Ying was still in Chang’an under the Gengshi regime. Fang Wang of Pingling and others, versed in the stars, believed Gengshi would fall and that Ying was the rightful heir; they rose in arms, escorted him to Linjing, and proclaimed him emperor.

sentences.zh: 更始遣丞相李松擊破殺嬰云。

translations.literal: Gengshi sent Chancellor Li Song to attack, defeat, and kill Ying, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi’s chancellor Li Song crushed the rising and killed Ying, or so the record runs.

sentences.zh: 東平思王宇

translations.literal: Prince Si of Dongping, Yu
translations.idiomatic: Prince Si of Dongping, Yu

sentences.zh: 東平思王宇，甘露二年立。

translations.literal: Prince Si of Dongping, Yu was established in the second year of Ganlu.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Si of Dongping, Yu, was enfeoffed in the second year of Ganlu (52 BCE).

sentences.zh: 元帝即位，就國。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan took the throne, he went to his kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: When Yuan became emperor, Yu proceeded to his fief.

sentences.zh: 壯大，通姦犯法，上以至親貰弗罪，傅相連坐。

translations.literal: When he grew to maturity he committed adultery and broke the law; the emperor, because he was closest kin, pardoned him without punishment, but the tutor and chancellor suffered joint liability.
translations.idiomatic: Once grown, he fornicated and broke the law; the emperor spared him as kin, but his tutor and minister paid for it with their posts.

sentences.zh: 久之，事太后，內不相得，太后上書言之，求守杜陵園。

translations.literal: After a long time, in serving the empress dowager they were not harmonious within; the empress dowager submitted a memorial stating it and asked to guard the park at Du Mausoleum.
translations.idiomatic: In time he and the empress dowager fell out; she memorialized the throne and asked leave to retire to tend the Du imperial tombs.

sentences.zh: 上於是遣太中大夫張子蟜奉璽書敕諭之，曰：「皇帝問東平王。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon sent Grand Counsellor of the Palace Zhang Zijiao bearing an imperial missive to admonish him, saying, “The emperor inquires of the King of Dongping.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then sent Palace Grandee Zhang Zijiao with a sealed edict: “The Son of Heaven addresses the king of Dongping.

sentences.zh: 蓋聞親親之恩莫重於孝，尊尊之義莫大於忠，故諸侯在位不驕以致孝道，制節謹度以冀天子，然後富貴不離於身，而社稷可保。

translations.literal: We have heard that in the kindness of cherishing kin nothing is weightier than filial piety, and in the duty of honoring the honored nothing is greater than loyalty; therefore the feudal kings in their positions do not act arrogantly so as to realize filial piety, restrain themselves and carefully keep measure so as to look to the Son of Heaven—then wealth and nobility do not leave their persons and the altars may be preserved.
translations.idiomatic: They say nothing binds kin like filial piety, nothing honors the throne like loyalty: a prince must curb pride to show filial duty, watch his bounds to await the emperor’s will—only then does fortune stay with him and the state remain secure.

sentences.zh: 今聞王自修有闕，本朝不和，流言紛紛，謗自內興，朕甚僭焉，為王懼之。

translations.literal: Now We hear the king’s self-cultivation has shortcomings, harmony with the capital is lacking, floating rumors swarm, slander arises from within—We are deeply distressed by this and fear for the king.
translations.idiomatic: Word reaches me that your conduct has slipped, harmony with the court has failed, rumor runs wild, and slander rises under your own roof—I am deeply troubled and afraid for you.

sentences.zh: 《詩》不雲乎？

translations.literal: Does not the Odes say?
translations.idiomatic: Does not the Classic of Poetry say?

sentences.zh: 『毋念爾祖，述修厥德，永言配命，自求多福』。

translations.literal: “Do not fix your thoughts on your ancestors; pursue and cultivate their virtue; forever align with Heaven’s charge; seek much blessing for yourself.”
translations.idiomatic: “Think not only of your forbears; carry forward their virtue; ever match Heaven’s mandate; win blessing by your own deeds.”

sentences.zh: 朕惟王之春秋方剛，忽於道德，意有所移，忠言未納，故臨遣太中大夫子蟜諭王朕意。

translations.literal: We consider the king’s spring and autumn to be in full vigor, that he has neglected moral power, his intentions have shifted, loyal words have not been accepted—therefore on the verge of dispatch We send Grand Counsellor of the Palace Zijiao to declare to the king Our intention.
translations.idiomatic: You are in the prime of life yet careless of virtue; you brush aside good counsel. I send Grandee Zijiao ahead of me to make my mind plain.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『過而不改，是謂過矣。』

translations.literal: Confucius said, “To err and not reform—that is what is called erring.”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “A fault uncorrected is a true fault.”

sentences.zh: 王其深惟孰思之，無違朕意。」

translations.literal: Let the king ponder deeply and think it through; do not disobey Our intention.”
translations.idiomatic: Reflect long and hard, my cousin, and do not set yourself against my will.”

sentences.zh: 又特以璽書賜王太后，曰：「皇帝使諸吏宦者令承問東平王太后。

translations.literal: In addition he specially with an imperial missive bestowed on the king’s mother, saying, “The emperor sends the Director of the Palace Attendants and Eunuchs to convey inquiry to the mother of the King of Dongping.
translations.idiomatic: He also sent a personal edict to the king’s mother: “I charge the chief of household attendants to greet the queen mother of Dongping.

sentences.zh: 朕有聞，王太后少加意焉。

translations.literal: We have heard; let the king’s mother give a little attention.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard certain things; the queen mother should weigh them carefully.

sentences.zh: 夫福善之門莫美於和睦，患咎之首莫大於內離。

translations.literal: For the gate of blessing and goodness nothing is finer than harmony; for the head of calamity and fault nothing is greater than inner estrangement.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing opens the door to blessing like concord at home; nothing breeds disaster like a split between mother and son.

sentences.zh: 今東平王出襁褓之中而托於南面之位，以年齒方剛，涉學日寡，驁忽臣下，不自它於太后，以是之間，能無失禮義者，其唯聖人乎！

translations.literal: Now the King of Dongping has come out of swaddling clothes and been entrusted with a south-facing seat; because his years and teeth are in full vigor, his study day by day is scant, he is proud and neglects ministers and officials, does not humble himself before the empress dowager—in such a gap, can one fail ritual and righteousness except the sage!
translations.idiomatic: The king left the cradle for a throne; he is young, barely schooled, and treats his officers with contempt while slighting his mother—only a sage could thread that needle without fault.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：『父為子隱，直在其中矣。』

translations.literal: The tradition says, “The father conceals for the son; uprightness is therein.”
translations.idiomatic: The tradition runs, “A father shields a son’s faults, and therein lies justice.”

sentences.zh: 王太后明察此意，不可不詳。

translations.literal: The king’s mother should clearly examine this meaning; she cannot fail to be thorough.
translations.idiomatic: Queen mother, read this meaning clearly and leave no corner unexamined.

sentences.zh: 閨門之內，母子之間，同氣異息，骨肉之恩，豈可忽哉！

translations.literal: Within the inner gates, between mother and son, same breath yet different exhalations—the kindness of bone and flesh—how can it be neglected!
translations.idiomatic: Within the women’s quarters, between mother and child, one flesh and blood—how can you cast that bond aside?

sentences.zh: 豈可忽哉！

translations.literal: How can it be neglected!
translations.idiomatic: You must not neglect it!

sentences.zh: 昔周公戒伯禽曰：『故舊無大故，則不可棄也，毋求備於一人。』

translations.literal: In antiquity the Duke of Zhou admonished Bo Qin, saying, “Unless old associates have great fault, they must not be discarded; do not demand completeness in one person.”
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Zhou warned Bo Qin: “Do not cast off old friends without grave cause; do not ask perfection of any single man.”

sentences.zh: 夫以故舊之恩，猶忍小惡，而況此乎！

translations.literal: By the kindness of old associates one still bears with small faults—how much more in this case!
translations.idiomatic: For old friends’ sake we forgive small slips—how much more between mother and son!

sentences.zh: 已遣使者諭王，王既悔過服罪，太后寬忍以貰之，後宜不敢。

translations.literal: We have already dispatched envoys to instruct the king; the king having repented faults and submitted to guilt, let the empress dowager be lenient and patient and pardon him; afterward he should not dare again.
translations.idiomatic: I have sent envoys to the king; he admits fault. Forgive him, madam, and he will not dare repeat it.

sentences.zh: 王太后強餐，止思念，慎疾自愛。」

translations.literal: Let the king’s mother force herself to eat, stop anxious thoughts, be careful of illness and love herself.”
translations.idiomatic: Eat for strength, madam, set worry aside, guard your health, and cherish yourself.”

sentences.zh: 字慚俱，因使者頓首謝死罪，願灑心自改。

translations.literal: Yu was ashamed and afraid; through the envoy he knocked his head and begged pardon for a crime deserving death, wishing to cleanse his heart and reform himself.
translations.idiomatic: Yu was stricken with shame and fear; through the envoy he kowtowed for a capital crime and vowed to cleanse his heart and mend his ways.

sentences.zh: 詔書又敕傅相曰：「夫人之性皆有五常，及其少長，耳目牽於耆欲，故五常銷而邪心作，情亂其性，利勝其義，而不失厥家者，未之有也。

translations.literal: An edict again instructed the tutor and chancellor, saying, “In human nature all have the five constants; when they grow from youth to adulthood, ears and eyes are pulled by fond desires, therefore the five constants wear away and wicked hearts arise, emotion disorders nature, profit overcomes righteousness—and yet not to lose one’s house: there has never been such a case.
translations.idiomatic: Another edict told tutor and minister: “Every man is born with the five constant virtues, yet age and appetite pull him off course until desire corrupts nature and gain drowns duty—none who so drift keep their house intact.

sentences.zh: 今王富於春秋，氣力勇武，獲師傅之教淺，加以少所聞見，自今以來，非《五經》之正術，敢以遊獵非禮道王者，輒以名聞。」

translations.literal: Now the king is rich in spring and autumn, his vigor is brave and martial, what he has received from tutor and teacher is shallow, and in addition he has heard and seen little; from now on, whoever dares to lead the king in hunting excursions by ways not of ritual that are not the orthodox arts of the Five Classics, immediately report the name.”
translations.idiomatic: The king is young, strong, and lightly taught, with narrow experience. Henceforth, if anyone leads him hunting or down unorthodox paths outside the Five Classics, report that man’s name at once.”

sentences.zh: 宇立二十年，元帝崩。

translations.literal: Yu had been established twenty years when Emperor Yuan died.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty years after Yu took the throne, Yuan died.

sentences.zh: 宇謂中謁者信等曰：「漢大臣議天子少弱，未能治天下，以為我知文法，建欲使我輔佐天子。

translations.literal: Yu said to the palace usher Xin and others, “Han ministers have deliberated that the Son of Heaven is young and weak and unable to govern the realm; they consider that I understand statutes and precedents, and propose that I assist the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Yu told the usher Xin and others, “The great ministers think the boy emperor too weak to rule and, because I know the statutes, mean to set me at his elbow.

sentences.zh: 我見尚書晨夜極苦，使我為之，不能也。

translations.literal: I have seen how the secretariat works morning and night in extreme toil; were it made me to do it, I could not.
translations.idiomatic: I have watched the masters of writing toil day and night—I could never bear that office.

sentences.zh: 今暑熱，縣官年少，持服恐無處所，我危得之！」

translations.literal: Now it is hot; the Son of Heaven is young; when he dons mourning I fear there will be nowhere to turn—I could nearly obtain it!”
translations.idiomatic: The weather is stifling, the throne is young, and when the court goes into mourning it may have no pillar—I could step into the breach!”

sentences.zh: 比至下，宇凡三哭，飲酒食肉，妻妾不離側。

translations.literal: By the time the edict came down, Yu had wept three times in all, drank wine and ate meat, and wives and concubines did not leave his side.
translations.idiomatic: When the death edict arrived, Yu wept three times for show, then feasted with wine and meat while his women never left his couch.

sentences.zh: 又姬朐臑故親幸，後疏遠，數歎息呼天。

translations.literal: Also his concubine Qu Nai had formerly been favored; afterward she was estranged and several times sighed and called to Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: A former favorite, the concubine Qu Nai, had been cast aside; she sighed to heaven again and again.

sentences.zh: 宇聞，斥朐臑為家人子，掃除永巷，數笞擊之。

translations.literal: When Yu heard, he demoted Qu Nai to the rank of family servant, set her to sweeping the long lane, and several times flogged her.
translations.idiomatic: Yu heard it, reduced her to menial rank, put her to sweeping the palace lane, and beat her repeatedly.

sentences.zh: 朐臑私疏宇過失，數令家告之。

translations.literal: Qu Nai privately drafted a list of Yu’s faults and several times had her family report them.
translations.idiomatic: She drafted a secret catalogue of his misdeeds and had kin report them upstream.

sentences.zh: 宇覺知，絞殺朐臑。

translations.literal: When Yu perceived it, he strangled Qu Nai to death.
translations.idiomatic: When Yu found out, he strangled her.

sentences.zh: 有司奏請逮捕，有詔削樊、亢父二縣。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized requesting arrest; there was an edict stripping Fan and Kangfu, two counties.
translations.idiomatic: Officials asked for his arrest; the emperor instead stripped two counties—Fan and Kangfu—from his fief.

sentences.zh: 後三歲，天子詔有司曰：「蓋聞仁以親親，古之道也。

translations.literal: After three years the Son of Heaven ordered the responsible officials, saying, “We have heard that benevolence lies in cherishing kin—this is the way of antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: Three years later the emperor told his officers: “Kindness begins with kin—that was the way of the ancients.

sentences.zh: 前東平王有闕，有司請廢，朕不忍。

translations.literal: Formerly the King of Dongping had faults; the responsible officials asked to depose him; We could not bear it.
translations.idiomatic: When Dongping erred before, some urged me to depose him; I could not.

sentences.zh: 又請削，朕不敢專。

translations.literal: They again asked to strip territory; We did not dare decide alone.
translations.idiomatic: Others asked to shave his domain; I would not act unilaterally.

sentences.zh: 惟王之至親，未嘗忘於心。

translations.literal: Only as to the king’s closest kin, We have never forgotten it in Our heart.
translations.idiomatic: Yet you are bone of my bone—I have not forgotten you.

sentences.zh: 今聞王改行自新，尊修經術，親近仁人，非法之求，不以奸吏，朕甚嘉焉。

translations.literal: Now We hear the king has changed conduct and renewed himself, honors and cultivates the classics, draws near to benevolent men, and does not employ crafty clerks for unlawful requests—We greatly approve.
translations.idiomatic: Word comes that you have mended your ways, take the classics seriously, keep company with good men, and no longer let sharp clerks peddle illicit favors—I applaud that.

sentences.zh: 傳不雲乎？

translations.literal: Does not the tradition say?
translations.idiomatic: Does not the tradition say?

sentences.zh: 朝過夕改，君子與之。

translations.literal: Fault in the morning and reform by evening—the gentleman approves.
translations.idiomatic: Wrong at dawn and right by dusk wins the gentleman’s praise.

sentences.zh: 其復前所削縣如故。」

translations.literal: Let him restore the former stripped counties as before.”
translations.idiomatic: Restore the counties I once took away.”

sentences.zh: 後年來朝，上疏求諸子及《太史公書》，上以問大將軍王鳳，對曰：「臣聞諸侯朝聘，考文章，正法度，非禮不言。

translations.literal: The next year he came to court and submitted a memorial requesting the Masters texts and the Grand Scribe’s Records; the emperor thereupon asked Grand General Wang Feng, who replied, “Your subject has heard that when feudal kings attend court they examine literary patterns and correct standards of law; they do not speak what is not ritual.
translations.idiomatic: The following year he attended court and asked for the “masters” compendia and Sima Qian’s history. The emperor consulted Wang Feng, who answered, “When kings come to audience they are to polish their style and law, not to chatter outside ritual.

sentences.zh: 今東平王幸得來朝，不思制節謹度，以防危失，而求諸書，非朝聘之義也。

translations.literal: Now the King of Dongping has been fortunate enough to come to court yet does not think of restraining himself and carefully keeping measure to guard against danger and loss, but seeks books—this is not the meaning of court attendance.
translations.idiomatic: Dongping should be tightening his belt, not courting danger by begging for books—that is not what a court visit is for.

sentences.zh: 諸子書或反經術，非聖人；

translations.literal: Among the Masters texts some oppose the classics and are not sages;
translations.idiomatic: Some of those miscellanies subvert the classics and fall short of the sages;

sentences.zh: 或明鬼神，信物怪；

translations.literal: some make clear ghosts and spirits and trust in prodigies;
translations.idiomatic: others retail ghosts, omens, and the uncanny;

sentences.zh: 《太史公書》有戰國縱橫權譎之謀，漢興之初謀臣奇策，天官災異，地形厄塞：皆不宜在諸侯王。

translations.literal: the Grand Scribe’s Records has Warring States vertical-and-horizontal cunning stratagems, strange plans of founding ministers at the rise of Han, celestial offices and disaster anomalies, terrain with narrow passes: all are unsuitable for feudal kings.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Scribe’s Records holds Warring States intrigue, founding conspiracies, astrology, disaster lore, and maps of strategic ground—none of it fit for a feudal king’s library.

sentences.zh: 不可予。

translations.literal: They must not be granted.
translations.idiomatic: They must not be granted.

sentences.zh: 不許之辭宜曰：『《五經》聖人所制，萬事靡不畢載。

translations.literal: The wording of refusal should say, “The Five Classics were made by sages; myriad affairs are therein fully recorded.
translations.idiomatic: Your refusal should read: “The Five Classics, fashioned by the sages, hold everything worth knowing.

sentences.zh: 王審樂道，傅相皆儒者，旦夕講誦，足以正身虞意。

translations.literal: If the king examines and delights in the Way, tutor and chancellor are all Confucians; morning and evening they lecture and recite—enough to correct the body and settle the mind.
translations.idiomatic: If my cousin loves the Way, his tutors are Ru scholars who can drill him dawn to dusk until mind and conduct are straight.

sentences.zh: 夫小辯破義，小道不通，致遠恐泥，皆不足以留意。

translations.literal: Petty disputation breaks righteousness, petty paths do not reach far—to go far one fears miring; all are insufficient to fix attention on.
translations.idiomatic: Small cleverness breaks the great pattern; side paths lead nowhere—fixing on them only mires the traveler.

sentences.zh: 諸益於經術者，不愛於王。』」

translations.literal: Whatever benefits the classics and techniques, We do not begrudge the king.”
translations.idiomatic: Whatever serves the classics, I do not withhold from you.”

sentences.zh: 對奏，天子如鳳言，遂不與。

translations.literal: When the reply was memorialized, the Son of Heaven followed Feng’s words and therefore did not grant them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted Feng’s memorial and denied the books.

sentences.zh: 立三十三年薨，子煬王雲嗣。

translations.literal: He was established thirty-three years and died; his son King Yang Yun succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He reigned thirty-three years and died; his son Yun, posthumously Yang, succeeded.

sentences.zh: 哀帝時，無鹽危山土自起覆草，如馳道狀，又瓠山石轉立。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Ai, at Danger Mountain in Wuyan earth rose of itself and covered grass in the shape of an imperial thoroughfare; also at Gourd Mountain a stone turned and stood upright.
translations.idiomatic: In Emperor Ai’s reign soil at Wuyan’s Danger Mountain heaved up over the turf in the shape of an imperial causeway, and a boulder at Gourd Mountain rolled upright.

sentences.zh: 雲及後謁自之石所祭，治石像瓠山立石，束倍草，並祠之。

translations.literal: Yun and his queen Ye went in person to the stone place to sacrifice, worked a stone in the image of the Gourd Mountain standing stone, tied double grass, and jointly sacrificed to them.
translations.idiomatic: Yun and Queen Ye visited the omens, set up a stone like the one at Gourd Mountain, bound paired stalks of grass, and offered joint sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 建平三年，息夫躬、孫寵等共因幸臣董賢告之。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianping, Xi Fu Gong, Sun Chong, and others together through the favored minister Dong Xian reported it.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianping 3, Xi Fu Gong, Sun Chong, and others denounced them through the favorite Dong Xian.

sentences.zh: 是時，哀帝被疾，多所惡，事下有司，逮王、後謁下獄驗治，言使巫傅恭、婢合歡等祠祭詛祝上，為雲求為天子。

translations.literal: At that time Emperor Ai was afflicted with illness and had many taboos; the matter was handed to the responsible officials, who arrested the king and Queen Ye and put them in prison for examination; it was said they had shamans Fu Gong and the maid Hehuan and others sacrifice and curse-pray for the emperor, seeking the kingship for Yun.
translations.idiomatic: Ai was ill and ill-tempered; officers jailed king and queen and proved they had set shamans Fu Gong and the slave girl Hehuan cursing the throne so Yun might become Son of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 雲又與知災異者高尚等指星宿，言上疾必不愈，雲當得天下。

translations.literal: Yun also with one who knew disasters and anomalies, Gao Shang and others, pointed to asterisms, saying the emperor’s illness would surely not improve and Yun ought to obtain the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Yun and omen-readers such as Gao Shang traced the stars, swearing the emperor would not recover and the mandate would fall to Yun.

sentences.zh: 石立，宣帝起之表也。

translations.literal: The stone standing upright was the sign of Emperor Xuan’s rise.
translations.idiomatic: The standing stone had been the omen of Emperor Xuan’s ascent.

sentences.zh: 有司請誅王，有詔廢徙房陵。

translations.literal: The responsible officials asked to execute the king; there was an edict deposing him and moving him to Fangling.
translations.idiomatic: Some demanded the king’s death; the edict spared his life but deposed him and banished him to Fangling.

sentences.zh: 雲自殺，謁棄市。

translations.literal: Yun killed himself; Ye was exposed in the market.
translations.idiomatic: Yun committed suicide; Ye was executed in the marketplace.

sentences.zh: 立十七年，國除。

translations.literal: He had been established seventeen years; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Seventeen years into his reign the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 元始元年，王莽欲反哀帝政，白太皇太后，立雲太子開明為東平王，又立思王孫成都為中山王。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yuanshi, Wang Mang wished to reverse Emperor Ai’s policies, reported to the Grand Empress Dowager, established Kai Ming, heir of Yun, as King of Dongping, and also established Chengdu, grandson of Prince Si, as King of Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanshi 1, Wang Mang, undoing Ai’s policies, persuaded the grand empress dowager to make Yun’s heir Kai Ming king of Dongping and to enfeoff Chengdu, grandson of the Prince of Dongping, as king of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 開明立三年，薨，無子。

translations.literal: Kai Ming was established three years and died; he had no son.
translations.idiomatic: Kai Ming reigned three years and died without issue.

sentences.zh: 復立開明兄嚴鄉侯信子匡為東平王，奉開明後。

translations.literal: They again established Kuang, son of Marquis Yanxiang Xin, as King of Dongping, to continue Kai Ming’s line.
translations.idiomatic: The court then set Kuang, son of Marquis Yanxiang Xin, on the Dongping throne to continue Kai Ming’s line.

sentences.zh: 王莽居攝，東郡太守翟義與嚴鄉侯信謀舉兵誅莽，立信為天子。

translations.literal: When Wang Mang held regency, Governor of the Eastern Region Zhai Yi plotted with Marquis Yanxiang Xin to raise troops to execute Mang and established Xin as Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Under Mang’s regency the eastern governor Zhai Yi conspired with Marquis Xin to march against Mang and proclaimed Xin emperor.

sentences.zh: 兵敗，皆為莽所滅。

translations.literal: The army was defeated; all were destroyed by Mang.
translations.idiomatic: Their forces were crushed and Mang wiped them out.

sentences.zh: 中山哀王竟

translations.literal: Prince Ai of Zhongshan, Jing
translations.idiomatic: Prince Ai of Zhongshan, Jing

sentences.zh: 中山哀王竟，初元二年立為清河王。

translations.literal: Prince Ai of Zhongshan, Jing, in the second year of Chuyuan was established as King of Qinghe.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Ai of Zhongshan, Jing, was made king of Qinghe in Chuyuan 2.

sentences.zh: 三年，徙中山，以幼少未之國。

translations.literal: In the third year he was moved to Zhongshan; because he was young and small he did not go to the kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: The next year he was transferred to Zhongshan but, still a child, did not take up the fief.

sentences.zh: 建昭四年，薨邸，葬杜陵，無子，絕。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Jianzhao he died in the lodge, was buried at Du Mausoleum, had no son, and the line was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: He died at the princely residence in Jianzhao 4 and was buried at Du; without an heir the line ended.

sentences.zh: 太后歸居外家戎氏。

translations.literal: The empress dowager returned to dwell with her maternal Rong clan.
translations.idiomatic: His mother the queen dowager went back to live with her Rong kin.

sentences.zh: 孝元皇帝三男

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoyuan had three sons
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan had three sons

sentences.zh: 孝元皇帝三男。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoyuan had three sons.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan had three sons.

sentences.zh: 王皇后生孝成帝，傅昭儀生定陶共王康，馮昭儀生中山孝王興。

translations.literal: Empress Wang bore Emperor Xiaocheng; Lady Fu the Brilliant Companion bore Prince Gong of Dingtao, Kang; Lady Feng the Brilliant Companion bore Prince Xiao of Zhongshan, Xing.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Wang gave birth to Emperor Cheng. Lady Fu the Brilliant Companion bore Prince Gong of Dingtao, Kang; Lady Feng the Brilliant Companion bore Prince Xiao of Zhongshan, Xing.

sentences.zh: 定陶共王康

translations.literal: Prince Gong of Dingtao, Kang
translations.idiomatic: Prince Gong of Dingtao, Kang

sentences.zh: 定陶共王康，永光三年立為濟陽王。

translations.literal: Prince Gong of Dingtao, Kang, in the third year of Yongguang was established as King of Jiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Gong of Dingtao, Kang, was enfeoffed as king of Jiyang in the third year of Yongguang.

sentences.zh: 八年，徙為山陽王。

translations.literal: In the eighth year he was moved to be King of Shanyang.
translations.idiomatic: In the eighth year he was transferred to Shanyang.

sentences.zh: 八年，徙定陶。

translations.literal: In the eighth year he was moved to Dingtao.
translations.idiomatic: He was then transferred to Dingtao.

sentences.zh: 王少而愛，長多材藝，習知音聲，上奇器之。

translations.literal: The king from youth was loved; grown, he had many talents and arts, was practiced in knowing music and sounds, and the emperor regarded him as extraordinary.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor doted on him from boyhood; grown, he was versatile and musical, and the throne marked him as exceptional.

sentences.zh: 母昭儀又幸，幾代皇后太子。

translations.literal: The mother Brilliant Companion was also favored and several times nearly replaced the empress and heir.
translations.idiomatic: His mother the brilliant companion was also favored and several times nearly unseated empress and crown prince.

sentences.zh: 語在《元后》及《史丹傳》。

translations.literal: The account is in the “Empress Yuan” and “Shi Dan” biographies.
translations.idiomatic: The story is told in the biographies of Empress Yuan and Shi Dan.

sentences.zh: 中山孝王興

translations.literal: Prince Xiao of Zhongshan, Xing
translations.idiomatic: Prince Xiao of Zhongshan, Xing

sentences.zh: 中山孝王興，建昭二年立為信都王。

translations.literal: Prince Xiao of Zhongshan, Xing, in the second year of Jianzhao was established as King of Xindu.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Xiao of Zhongshan, Xing, was made king of Xindu in Jianzhao 2.

sentences.zh: 十四年，徙中山。

translations.literal: In the fourteenth year he was moved to Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourteenth year he was transferred to Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 成帝之議立太子也，御史大夫孔光以為《尚書》有殷及王，兄終弟及，中山王元帝之子，宜為後。

translations.literal: When Emperor Cheng deliberated establishing the heir apparent, Imperial Counselor Kong Guang thought the Documents had Yin and royal houses where the elder brother ended and the younger brother succeeded; the King of Zhongshan was Emperor Yuan’s son and ought to be successor.
translations.idiomatic: When Cheng debated the succession, Imperial Counselor Kong Guang cited the Documents on Yin brother-to-brother transmission and argued that Xing, Yuan’s son and king of Zhongshan, should follow.

sentences.zh: 成帝以中山王不材，又兄弟，不得相入廟。

translations.literal: Emperor Cheng considered the King of Zhongshan untalented and also that brothers could not enter one another’s temples.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng judged Xing unfit and held that brothers could not share one temple line.

sentences.zh: 外家王氏與趙昭儀皆欲用哀帝為太子，故遂立焉。

translations.literal: The maternal Wang clan and Lady Zhao the Brilliant Companion both wished to use Emperor Ai as heir apparent; therefore he was finally established.
translations.idiomatic: The Wangs at court and Lady Zhao the brilliant companion both wanted Ai as heir, so he was chosen.

sentences.zh: 上乃封孝王舅馮參為宜鄉侯，而益封孝王萬戶，以尉其意。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon enfeoffed Prince Xiao’s maternal uncle Feng Can as Marquis of Yixiang and increased Prince Xiao’s fief by ten thousand households to comfort his mind.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then made Xing’s uncle Feng Can marquis of Yixiang and added ten thousand households to Xing’s fief to soothe him.

sentences.zh: 三十年，薨，子衎嗣。

translations.literal: In the thirtieth year he died; his son Kan succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the thirtieth year of his reign; his son Kan inherited.

sentences.zh: 七年，哀帝崩，無子，征中山王衎入即位，是為平帝。

translations.literal: In the seventh year Emperor Ai died without a son; they summoned the King of Zhongshan Kan to enter and take the throne—this was Emperor Ping.
translations.idiomatic: In the seventh year of Kan’s reign Ai died childless; Kan was summoned to the capital as Emperor Ping.

sentences.zh: 太皇太后以帝為成帝後，故立東平思王孫桃鄉頃侯子成都為中山王，奉孝王後。

translations.literal: The Grand Empress Dowager, because the emperor was to continue Cheng’s line, therefore established Chengdu, son of Marquis Qing of Taoxiang, grandson of Prince Si of Dongping, as King of Zhongshan, to continue Prince Xiao’s line.
translations.idiomatic: The grand empress dowager, treating Ping as Cheng’s heir, enfeoffed Chengdu—son of the Marquis of Taoxiang and great-grandson of Prince Si of Dongping—as king of Zhongshan to carry on Xing’s line.

sentences.zh: 王莽時絕。

translations.literal: At the time of Wang Mang the line was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: The line ended under Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：孝元之後，遍有天下，然而世絕於孫，豈非天哉！

translations.literal: The eulogy says: After Emperor Xiaoyuan, they broadly held the realm, yet the generations were cut off at the grandson—is it not Heaven!
translations.idiomatic: The summation runs: from Emperor Yuan onward they held all under heaven, yet the line died with the grandson—can we call it anything but fate?

sentences.zh: 淮陽憲王於時諸侯為聰察矣，張博誘之，幾陷無道。

translations.literal: Prince Xian of Huaiyang was among the feudal lords the most perceptive; Zhang Bo enticed him and he nearly fell into lack of the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Xian of Huaiyang was the sharpest of the kings of his day; Zhang Bo led him to the brink of treason.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云「貪人敗類」，古今一也。

translations.literal: The Odes says, “The greedy man destroys his kind”—ancient and modern are one.
translations.idiomatic: The Poetry says, “The grasping man ruins his own”—so it has ever been.
